title: Senate Votes to Keep Spy Program for a Little While
summary: The U.S. Senate has voted to keep a spy program called FISA running until April 30. This program helps spy agencies watch people to keep the country safe, but some people think it's not fair because it might invade privacy. At the same time, big storms are hitting the Midwest, causing damage and setting rain records. In another story, an 85-year-old woman who was held in immigration custody has gone back home. These events show how the government makes decisions and how weather can affect people's lives.

options: Change it to a new law

What record did Milwaukee set in April?
Who has returned home after being held in custody?
How did the Senate vote on the FISA renewal?

background_read: The U.S. government has different parts, like the Senate, that make laws. FISA is a law from 1978 that allows agencies to watch people suspected of being threats, but it's debated because it might not respect privacy. Weather events like storms can cause damage and records, like in Milwaukee. Immigration involves rules for people entering the U.S., and sometimes elderly people get caught up in it.
Article_Structure: WHO: The U.S. Senate and people in the Midwest
WHAT: Voting to renew a spy program and dealing with severe storms
WHERE: In the U.S., especially the Midwest and Washington D.C.
WHY: To keep the country safe and report on weather and immigration issues

perspectives.description: Some people support the FISA renewal because it helps spy agencies protect the country from threats.
Others worry that the FISA program invades people's privacy and might be misused.
